_description: IPL coaches and support staff since 2008. Includes head coaches, assistant coaches, bowling/batting/fielding coaches, mentors, director of cricket, and support staff. Every tenure is cited. Entries with verified=false are flagged for user review.
_phase: All phases COMPLETE — Head coaches, support staff, defunct franchises, and Phase 3 (physios/trainers/analysts/team managers/CEOs/doctors).

coaches.name: Kepler Wessels
coaches.playingCareerSummary: Former international batter for Australia (1982-85, 24 Tests, 54 ODIs) and South Africa (1991-94, 16 Tests, 55 ODIs). Captained South Africa on their post-isolation return. Did not play in the IPL.
coaches.bio: The inaugural head coach of Chennai Super Kings, appointed before the 2008 IPL season after a distinguished playing career for both Australia and South Africa. Led CSK to the final of the very first IPL under captain MS Dhoni, where they narrowly lost to Rajasthan Royals. He was replaced by Stephen Fleming after a single season.

perSeason.notes: Finished 3rd in league (16 pts). Beat Kings XI Punjab by 9 wickets in semi-final. Lost final to Rajasthan Royals by 3 wickets.

otherCoachingRoles.notes: Head coach of the Proteas senior men's team.
Multiple domestic T20 and first-class titles in South Africa.

coaches.name: Stephen Fleming
coaches.playingCareerSummary: Former New Zealand captain (1997-2006) and one of the most respected tactical minds of his generation: 111 Tests, 280 ODIs for NZ. Played for Chennai Super Kings in the inaugural 2008 IPL season before retiring and taking over as head coach in 2009.
coaches.bio: The most successful head coach in IPL history by titles won (5). Took charge of Chennai Super Kings in 2009 and has led them through every season of the franchise's existence barring 2016 and 2017, when CSK was suspended — during that period he coached Rising Pune Supergiant, taking them to the 2017 final (lost to MI by 1 run). Has guided CSK to five IPL titles (2010, 2011, 2018, 2021, 2023). Known for a calm, methodical tactical style and a remarkable partnership with captain MS Dhoni.

perSeason.notes: Finished 2nd in league stage (17 pts). Lost semi-final to RCB by 6 wickets. 2009 format had no Q1/Q2; SF loss is listed as 'Qualifier 2' equivalent (eliminated in second playoff round).
Qualified for playoffs 3rd on NRR (14 pts, four-way tie). Beat Deccan Chargers in SF by 38 runs, beat Mumbai Indians in final by 22 runs. First CSK title.
Finished 2nd in league (18 pts). Beat RCB in Qualifier 1 by 6 wickets, beat RCB in final by 58 runs in Chennai. Second consecutive title.
Finished 4th in league (17 pts). Eliminator W vs MI, Qualifier 2 W vs DD, lost final to KKR by 5 wickets.
Finished top 2 in league. Qualifier 1 W vs MI by 48 runs, lost final to MI by 23 runs.
Finished 3rd in league (20 pts). Eliminator W vs MI by 7 wickets, Qualifier 2 L vs Kings XI Punjab by 24 runs.
Finished 1st in league (18 pts). Qualifier 1 L vs MI by 25 runs, Qualifier 2 W vs RCB by 3 wickets, lost final to MI by 41 runs.
Return from 2016-17 suspension. Finished 2nd in league (18 pts). Qualifier 1 W vs SRH by 2 wickets, final W vs SRH by 8 wickets (Shane Watson 117* Player of the Match). Third IPL title.
Finished 2nd (18 pts, tied with MI and DC on NRR). Qualifier 1 L vs MI by 6 wkts, Qualifier 2 W vs DC by 6 wkts, lost final to MI by 1 run.
First season CSK failed to qualify for playoffs. Finished 7th (12 pts). Played in UAE due to COVID-19.
Finished 2nd in league (18 pts). Qualifier 1 W vs DC by 4 wickets, final W vs KKR by 27 runs in Dubai. Fourth IPL title.
Finished 9th of 10 (8 pts). Jadeja stepped down as captain mid-season; Dhoni resumed leadership.
Finished 2nd (17 pts). Qualifier 1 W vs GT by 15 runs, final W vs GT by 5 wickets (DLS) in rain-affected three-day final. Fifth IPL title, equalling MI.
Missed playoffs on NRR tie-breaker. Finished 5th (14 pts). Dhoni handed captaincy to Ruturaj Gaikwad before the season.
Franchise-worst: finished 10th (last) with 8 pts — the first time CSK ended at the bottom of the table.
2026 season in progress — stats will update as matches complete.

tenures.notes: 2016-2017 seasons excluded: CSK was suspended from the IPL following the 2013 spot-fixing/betting investigation. Fleming coached Rising Pune Supergiant in those years — that tenure will be added in Phase 4 (defunct franchises). 2026 perSeason row is a placeholder while the ongoing season plays out.

perSeason.notes: Finished 7th of 8 with 10 points. Franchise's inaugural season during CSK's suspension.
Finished 2nd in league (20 pts). Beat MI in Qualifier 1 by 20 runs; lost final to MI by 1 run. Franchise's final season — disbanded when CSK returned in 2018. Captain: Steve Smith.

tenures.notes: Coached Rising Pune Supergiant during CSK's 2016-2017 suspension. Took them from 7th in 2016 to the 2017 final (lost to MI by 1 run) — one of the narrowest margins in IPL final history.

otherCoachingRoles.notes: Head coach of Nottinghamshire CCC; won the 2017 NatWest T20 Blast and One-Day Cup double.

coaches.name: Lalchand Rajput
coaches.playingCareerSummary: Former India opening batter (2 Tests, 4 ODIs, 1985-87). Manager of the India team that won the 2007 ICC World T20.
coaches.bio: The inaugural head coach of the Mumbai Indians in the 2008 IPL. He came in directly off the back of managing India's 2007 T20 World Cup-winning squad. Replaced by Shaun Pollock after a single season in which MI finished 5th.

perSeason.notes: Finished 5th of 8 with 14 points. Did not qualify for semi-finals. Franchise's inaugural season.

otherCoachingRoles.notes: Afghanistan national team head coach across two stints; guided their 2018 Asia Cup qualification campaign in his first stint.

coaches.name: Shaun Pollock
coaches.playingCareerSummary: Former South Africa captain and one of the most accurate pacers of his era: 108 Tests (421 wickets), 303 ODIs (393 wickets). Played for Mumbai Indians in the inaugural 2008 IPL season before retiring as a player and becoming their head coach in 2009.
coaches.bio: Took over as Mumbai Indians head coach in 2009 after retiring as a player post the 2008 IPL. His single season in charge was a difficult one — the tournament was relocated to South Africa and MI finished 7th. Was replaced by Robin Singh the following year but remained associated with the franchise as a bowling coach for a period afterwards.

perSeason.notes: Finished 7th of 8 with 11 points. Season played entirely in South Africa due to general elections in India.

coaches.name: Robin Singh
coaches.playingCareerSummary: Former India all-rounder (1 Test, 136 ODIs, 1989-2001) renowned as one of the finest fielders and finishers India has produced. Did not play in the IPL as his career ended in 2001.
coaches.bio: Two-stint Mumbai Indians support staff member: head coach 2010-2012 (taking MI to their first IPL final in 2010) and batting coach 2019-2022 (part of the title-winning sides in 2019 and 2020). In between, served as India's fielding coach and coached multiple international sides.

perSeason.notes: Finished last of 8 with 4 points despite being one of the pre-tournament favourites.

tenures.notes: Robin Singh's first IPL head coach role — took over Deccan Chargers for the inaugural 2008 season. Replaced by Darren Lehmann after a disastrous 8th-place finish.

perSeason.notes: Finished 1st in league (20 pts, NRR 1.084). Beat RCB in semi-final by 35 runs; lost final to CSK by 22 runs.
Finished 3rd in league (18 pts). Beat KKR in Eliminator by 4 wickets; lost Qualifier 2 to RCB by 43 runs.
Finished 4th in league (NRR -0.100). Lost Eliminator to CSK by 38 runs. Anil Kumble joined as chief mentor this season.
Finished 1st in league (18 pts, NRR 0.421). Beat CSK in Q1 by 6 wickets; beat CSK in final by 1 run. Franchise's fourth IPL title.
Finished 1st in league (18 pts). Beat DC in Q1 by 57 runs; beat DC in final by 5 wickets. Fifth IPL title. Season played in UAE due to COVID-19. Became 2nd team after CSK to defend an IPL title.
Finished 5th with 14 points. Could not defend their title.
Finished 10th (last) with 8 points and NRR -0.506. Franchise-worst season to that point.

tenures.notes: Returned to Mumbai Indians as batting coach after previously being head coach 2010-2012. Part of the 2019 and 2020 title-winning support staff.

otherCoachingRoles.notes: Fielding coach of the India senior men's team, including during the 2007 T20 World Cup winning campaign.

coaches.name: John Wright
coaches.playingCareerSummary: Former New Zealand opener and captain: 82 Tests (5334 runs), 149 ODIs. First foreign head coach of the India national team (2000-2005).
coaches.bio: Mumbai Indians head coach from 2013 to 2014. Delivered the franchise's maiden IPL title in 2013, guiding a side captained by Rohit Sharma in his first year as full-time captain. Followed it up with a 4th-place finish and Eliminator exit in 2014, after which Ricky Ponting took over.

perSeason.notes: Finished 2nd in league (22 pts). Lost Q1 to CSK by 48 runs; beat RR in Q2 by 4 wickets; beat CSK in final by 23 runs. Franchise's first IPL title. Sachin Tendulkar's final IPL season.
Finished 4th in league (14 pts, NRR 0.095). Lost Eliminator to CSK by 7 wickets.

tenures.notes: Head of Talent Scouting role under head coach Mahela Jayawardene.

otherCoachingRoles.notes: First foreign head coach of India; oversaw the 2003 World Cup final run.

coaches.name: Ricky Ponting
coaches.playingCareerSummary: Former Australia captain and one of the greatest Test batters of all time: 168 Tests (13,378 runs), 375 ODIs. 2-time ODI World Cup winning captain. Played for KKR in 2008 and MI in 2013 in the IPL.
coaches.bio: The most-tenured overseas coach in the IPL. Started his head-coaching career at Mumbai Indians (2015-2016), winning the 2015 title in his first season. Moved to Delhi in 2018 (Delhi Daredevils) and continued through the franchise's 2019 rebrand to Delhi Capitals, serving seven seasons and transforming them from serial under-achievers into consistent contenders, reaching the 2020 final (lost to MI) and topping the league in 2021. Parted ways with DC ahead of the 2025 mega auction and was appointed Punjab Kings head coach in September 2024 — his fourth IPL franchise role. Delivered PBKS's second-ever IPL final in 2025 (lost to RCB by 6 runs) in his first season. Retained for 2026.

perSeason.notes: Finished 2nd in league (16 pts). Beat CSK in Q1 by 25 runs; beat CSK in final by 41 runs. Franchise's second IPL title.
Finished 5th with 14 points. Did not qualify for playoffs.
Finished 8th (last) with 10 points (NRR -0.222). Ponting's first IPL head-coaching season after leaving MI. Final DD season before the franchise rebranded to Delhi Capitals for 2019.

tenures.notes: 2018 was the final season under the 'Delhi Daredevils' name before the franchise rebranded to Delhi Capitals in 2019.

perSeason.notes: Franchise reborn as Delhi Capitals. Finished 3rd (18 pts). Beat SRH in Eliminator by 2 wickets (first-ever franchise playoff win); lost Qualifier 2 to CSK by 6 wickets.

Finished 2nd (16 pts). Lost Qualifier 1 to MI by 57 runs; beat SRH in Qualifier 2 by 17 runs; lost final to MI by 5 wickets. Franchise's first-ever IPL final, 12 years after their 2008 debut. Played in UAE due to COVID-19.
Finished 1st in league (20 pts, NRR 0.481) — franchise-best. Lost Qualifier 1 to CSK by 4 wickets; lost Qualifier 2 to KKR by 3 wickets. Season split between India and UAE due to COVID-19.
Finished 5th in Group A with 14 points (NRR 0.204). Missed playoffs on NRR tie-breaker.
Finished 9th with 10 points (NRR -0.808). Lost first five matches of the season. Captain: David Warner.
Finished 6th with 14 points. Ponting's final season at DC; he and the franchise parted ways ahead of the 2025 mega auction.

Finished 1st in league (19 points, NRR 0.376) — franchise-best league finish since 2008. Lost Qualifier 1 to RCB; beat Mumbai Indians in Qualifier 2; lost final to RCB by 6 runs at Ahmedabad. Ponting's first season in charge; captain Shreyas Iyer. PBKS's second IPL final (first was 2014).
2026 season in progress — PBKS retained Ponting as head coach after the 2025 runner-up finish. Stats will update as matches complete.

coaches.name: Mahela Jayawardene
coaches.fullName: Denagamage Proboth Mahela de Silva Jayawardene
coaches.playingCareerSummary: Former Sri Lanka captain and one of the game's most elegant batters: 149 Tests (11,814 runs), 448 ODIs (12,650 runs). IPL playing career: Kings XI Punjab (2008), Kochi Tuskers Kerala (2011), Delhi Daredevils (2013-2015).
coaches.bio: The only person to win three IPL titles as Mumbai Indians head coach (2017, 2019, 2020). First joined as MI's batting consultant in 2015 before being elevated to head coach in 2017. Led MI through the most successful run in franchise history, including back-to-back titles in 2019-2020. Stepped down after the disastrous 2022 season and was replaced by Mark Boucher. Returned to the role in 2025 after Boucher's two seasons yielded limited success.

perSeason.notes: Finished 1st in league (20 pts). Lost Q1 to RPS by 20 runs; beat KKR in Q2 by 6 wickets; beat RPS in final by 1 run. Franchise's third IPL title.
Finished 5th with 12 points. Did not qualify for playoffs.
Finished 1st in league (18 pts, NRR 0.421). Beat CSK in Q1 by 6 wickets; beat CSK in final by 1 run. Franchise's fourth IPL title.
Finished 1st in league (18 pts). Beat DC in Q1 by 57 runs; beat DC in final by 5 wickets. Fifth IPL title. Season played in UAE due to COVID-19. Became 2nd team after CSK to defend an IPL title.
Finished 5th with 14 points. Could not defend their title.
Finished 10th (last) with 8 points and NRR -0.506. Franchise-worst season to that point.

tenures.notes: First stint. Three titles (2017, 2019, 2020) — second-most by any IPL head coach after Stephen Fleming.

perSeason.notes: Finished 4th in league (16 pts, NRR 1.142). Beat GT in Eliminator by 20 runs; lost Q2 to PBKS by 5 wickets.
2026 season in progress — stats will update as matches complete.

tenures.notes: Second stint — returned to replace Mark Boucher ahead of IPL 2025.

otherCoachingRoles.notes: Batting consultant with the England white-ball side during their 2019 ODI World Cup winning campaign.
Head coach of the Southern Brave men's team in The Hundred.

coaches.name: Mark Boucher
coaches.playingCareerSummary: Former South Africa wicket-keeper with a world-record 555 Test dismissals across 147 Tests. Played for Royal Challengers Bangalore and Kolkata Knight Riders in the IPL.
coaches.bio: Mumbai Indians head coach for 2023 and 2024. Replaced Mahela Jayawardene after the 2022 disaster and took MI back to the playoffs in his first season (lost Q2 to GT). His second season ended in another last-place finish, prompting MI to bring Jayawardene back for 2025.

perSeason.notes: Finished 4th in league. Beat LSG in Eliminator; lost Q2 to GT.
Finished 10th (last) with 8 points. First team eliminated from IPL 2024.

otherCoachingRoles.notes: Proteas head coach, including through the 2021 T20 World Cup. Resigned amid ongoing Social Justice and Nation Building Project proceedings.

coaches.name: Venkatesh Prasad
coaches.playingCareerSummary: Former India right-arm fast-medium bowler: 33 Tests (96 wickets), 161 ODIs (196 wickets). Famed for his duel with Aamer Sohail in the 1996 World Cup quarter-final. Did not play in the IPL (retired in 2005).
coaches.bio: Royal Challengers Bangalore's inaugural head coach in 2008. Oversaw a poor first campaign (7th of 8) before being replaced by Ray Jennings ahead of IPL 2009. Later returned to coaching as India's bowling coach under Greg Chappell and in various roles in domestic Indian cricket.

perSeason.notes: Finished 7th of 8 with 8 points. Martin Crowe briefly held the chief cricketing officer role earlier in the team's build-up before Prasad coached the inaugural IPL season.
Finished 7th with 12 points. Missed playoffs. Single-season tenure; Mike Hesson replaced him for 2019.

tenures.notes: Bowling coach under Brad Hodge. Appointed in March 2018.

otherCoachingRoles.notes: Bowling coach under head coaches Gary Kirsten and predecessors.
Short-term consultancy role.

coaches.name: Ray Jennings
coaches.playingCareerSummary: Former South African wicket-keeper who played first-class cricket in the pre-readmission era. Did not play international cricket for SA due to apartheid-era boycott.
coaches.bio: Longest-serving RCB head coach from 2009 to 2013. Took RCB to their first IPL final in 2009 (lost to Deccan Chargers) and a second in 2011 (lost to CSK). Delivered three playoff appearances in five seasons but failed to secure a title. Replaced by Daniel Vettori ahead of 2014.

perSeason.notes: Finished 4th in league (14 pts, NRR 0.219, tied with CSK and DD). Lost semi-final; won 3rd-place playoff against Deccan Chargers — official tournament finish: 3rd.
Finished 1st in league (19 pts). Lost Qualifier 1 to CSK by 6 wickets; beat MI in Qualifier 2 by 43 runs; lost final to CSK by 58 runs. Gained the 'bridesmaid' tag that stuck for years.
Finished 5th with 17 points (NRR -0.022). Did not qualify for playoffs.
Finished 5th. Did not qualify for playoffs despite strong batting lineup.

coaches.name: Daniel Vettori
coaches.playingCareerSummary: Former New Zealand captain and left-arm spin great: 113 Tests (362 wickets), 295 ODIs (305 wickets). Played for Delhi Daredevils (2008-2011) and Royal Challengers Bangalore (2012-2014) in the IPL.
coaches.bio: Former New Zealand captain and IPL player, turned successful franchise head coach. RCB head coach from 2014 to 2018, taking them to the 2016 IPL final (lost to SRH by 8 runs). Moved to Sunrisers Hyderabad in August 2023 to replace Brian Lara and took them back to the IPL final in his first season (2024), losing to KKR. Currently also assistant coach of the Australia senior men's cricket team under Andrew McDonald.

perSeason.notes: Finished 7th with 10 points (NRR -0.428). First season under Vettori.
Finished 3rd with 16 points. Beat RR in Eliminator by 71 runs; lost Qualifier 2 to CSK by 3 wickets.
Finished 2nd with 16 points. Beat Gujarat Lions in Qualifier 1 by 4 wickets; lost final to Sunrisers Hyderabad by 8 runs at Chinnaswamy. Kohli's orange-cap season (973 runs).
Finished last (8th) with 7 points (NRR -1.299). Franchise-worst season to that point.
Finished 6th with 12 points. Gary Kirsten was promoted to head coach in August 2018 (after the season), replacing Vettori for 2019.

Finished 2nd in league (17 pts). Qualifier 1 loss to KKR; Qualifier 2 W vs Rajasthan Royals; lost final to KKR by 8 wickets. SRH's second IPL final after 2018. Vettori's first season replacing Brian Lara.
Finished 6th with 13 points. Third team eliminated from IPL 2025. Pat Cummins continued as captain.

2026 season in progress. Vettori retained; new spin bowling coach Muttiah Muralitharan added to the support staff alongside Hemang Badani as batting coach (his second IPL role alongside Delhi Capitals). Stats will update as the season progresses.

otherCoachingRoles.notes: Assistant coach under Andrew McDonald in the Australia senior men's setup.

coaches.name: Gary Kirsten
coaches.playingCareerSummary: Former South Africa opener: 101 Tests (7289 runs), 185 ODIs (6798 runs). Did not play in the IPL (retired from international cricket in 2004). Coached India to the 2011 ODI World Cup title.
coaches.bio: Long-serving international coach whose IPL head-coaching record spans two franchises. First took charge of Delhi Daredevils in 2014 (replacing Eric Simons) and oversaw the franchise-worst 2014 season (1-13) before an improved but still non-playoff 2015, after which he was sacked. Returned as RCB head coach for 2019 — a single non-playoff season — before leaving IPL head-coaching altogether. Later joined Gujarat Titans as mentor and coached Pakistan's white-ball side.

perSeason.notes: Finished 8th (last) with 2 points — franchise-worst season. Just one win all campaign. NRR -1.182.
Finished 7th. Kirsten was replaced after this season; Paddy Upton took over for 2016.
Finished 6th with 12 points. Gary Kirsten was promoted to head coach in August 2018 (after the season), replacing Vettori for 2019.

tenures.notes: Appointed batting coach in January 2018, promoted to head coach for 2019 season.

perSeason.notes: Finished 8th (last) with 11 points. Kirsten had been appointed batting coach in January 2018, promoted to head coach in August 2018 ahead of this season. Single season in charge before Simon Katich replaced him.

Finished 1st in league (20 pts, NRR 0.316). Beat Rajasthan Royals in Qualifier 1 by 7 wickets; beat RR in final by 7 wickets at Narendra Modi Stadium, Ahmedabad. Inaugural season title — GT became only the second team (after CSK in 2008) to win the IPL in their debut season. Captain: Hardik Pandya. Gary Kirsten: batting coach/mentor.

Finished 1st in league (20 pts). Lost Qualifier 1 to CSK by 15 runs; beat Mumbai Indians in Qualifier 2 by 62 runs; lost final to CSK by 5 wickets (DLS). The 'longest-ever IPL final' — spanned 3 calendar days due to rain, ending at 01:35 IST on 30 May.
Finished 8th with 12 points (NRR -1.063). First season GT missed the playoffs. Captain Shubman Gill took over from Hardik Pandya who returned to MI.

tenures.notes: Batting coach and mentor under Ashish Nehra for three seasons — part of the inaugural 2022 title-winning staff and the 2023 final campaign. Replaced by Parthiv Patel for 2025.

otherCoachingRoles.notes: Led India to the 2011 ODI World Cup and the world No.1 Test ranking.
Mentor in GT's inaugural title-winning season under Ashish Nehra.

coaches.name: Simon Katich
coaches.playingCareerSummary: Former Australia Test opener: 56 Tests (4188 runs), 45 ODIs. Played for Kings XI Punjab in the inaugural 2008 IPL season.
coaches.bio: RCB head coach from 2020 to 2021. Took over from Gary Kirsten and led a Kohli-led RCB back to consecutive playoff appearances — their first back-to-back playoff run since 2010-2011. Stepped down mid-2021 season citing personal reasons on his 46th birthday; Mike Hesson, the franchise's Director of Cricket, took over as interim head coach for the UAE leg.

perSeason.notes: Finished 5th with 13 points. Did not qualify for playoffs. Kallis was replaced by Brendon McCullum after this season.

tenures.notes: Assistant coach to Jacques Kallis at KKR for one season before becoming Royal Challengers Bangalore head coach in 2020.

perSeason.notes: Finished 4th with 14 points (NRR -0.172). Lost Eliminator to Sunrisers Hyderabad by 6 wickets. Played in UAE due to COVID-19.

Finished 3rd with 18 points. Katich stepped down mid-season citing personal reasons (August 21, 2021), after which Director of Cricket Mike Hesson took over as interim head coach for the UAE leg and the playoffs. Lost Eliminator to KKR by 4 wickets. Season split across India and UAE legs due to COVID-19.

tenures.notes: 2021 stats credited to Katich as he was head coach at the start of the season and through the suspended India leg. Hesson took over as interim HC for the UAE leg — Hesson's DoC role across 2020-2023 will be captured in Phase 2/3 (support staff).

coaches.name: Sanjay Bangar
coaches.playingCareerSummary: Former India all-rounder: 12 Tests, 15 ODIs (2001-2004). Played for Deccan Chargers (2008) and Kolkata Knight Riders (2009-2010) in the IPL. Widely respected technical batting coach post-retirement.
coaches.bio: Long-serving Indian domestic coach and former India batting coach. First IPL head-coaching role was with Kings XI Punjab (2014-2016), where his 2014 side reached the franchise's first-ever IPL final under captain George Bailey — losing to KKR by 3 wickets in a 200-run chase. KXIP collapsed to back-to-back last-place finishes in 2015 and 2016 and Bangar stepped down. He later returned to IPL head-coaching with Royal Challengers Bangalore (2022-2023), taking RCB to the playoffs in 2022 before missing them in 2023.

perSeason.notes: Finished 1st in league (22 pts). Lost Q1 to KKR; beat CSK in Q2 by 28 runs; lost final to KKR by 3 wickets (KKR chased 200). Franchise's first-ever IPL final. KXIP's best-ever season.
Finished 8th (last) with 6 points. Worst title defence in IPL history — went from champions-in-waiting to wooden spoon in 12 months. NRR -1.436.
Finished 8th (last) with 8 points (NRR -0.646). Bangar stepped down in December 2016.

Finished 3rd with 18 points. Katich stepped down mid-season citing personal reasons (August 21, 2021), after which Director of Cricket Mike Hesson took over as interim head coach for the UAE leg and the playoffs. Lost Eliminator to KKR by 4 wickets. Season split across India and UAE legs due to COVID-19.

tenures.notes: Batting consultant role under head coach Mike Hesson. Promoted to head coach for 2022.

perSeason.notes: Finished 4th. Beat LSG in Eliminator by 14 runs; lost Qualifier 2 to Rajasthan Royals by 7 wickets.
Finished 6th with 14 points. Did not qualify for playoffs. Replaced by Andy Flower ahead of IPL 2024.

otherCoachingRoles.notes: Long-serving India batting coach across multiple head coaches.
Batting coach under Simon Katich / Mike Hesson before being promoted to head coach in 2022.

coaches.name: Andy Flower
coaches.playingCareerSummary: Former Zimbabwe captain and wicket-keeper batter, widely regarded as Zimbabwe's greatest Test player: 63 Tests (4794 runs at 51.54), 213 ODIs (6786 runs). Did not play in the IPL. Brother of Grant Flower.
coaches.bio: Andy Flower's IPL coaching career began at Kings XI Punjab / Punjab Kings as assistant coach (2020-2021) under Anil Kumble, before taking over Lucknow Super Giants as their inaugural head coach (2022-2023). He then moved to Royal Challengers Bengaluru in 2024 and delivered their first-ever IPL title in 2025 — ending 18 years of RCB's title drought with a 6-run final win over Punjab Kings. Retained for 2026.

perSeason.notes: Finished 6th with 12 points. Final season under the 'Kings XI Punjab' name — the franchise rebranded to 'Punjab Kings' on 17 February 2021.

tenures.notes: Assistant coach under Anil Kumble. First IPL coaching role.

perSeason.notes: First season under the 'Punjab Kings' name. Finished 6th with 12 points.

tenures.notes: Continued as assistant coach into the rebranded Punjab Kings before moving to LSG for 2022.

perSeason.notes: Inaugural LSG season. Finished 4th in league (18 pts). Lost Eliminator to Royal Challengers Bangalore by 14 runs at Eden Gardens — Rajat Patidar 112* off 54. KL Rahul top-scored with 616 runs. Gautam Gambhir: mentor.
Finished 3rd in league (17 pts, NRR 0.284). Lost Eliminator to Mumbai Indians. Flower left after this season to join Royal Challengers Bengaluru for 2024.

tenures.notes: LSG's inaugural head coach — took the franchise to the Eliminator playoffs in both of their first two seasons before leaving to join RCB for IPL 2024.

perSeason.notes: Finished 4th with 14 points. Lost Eliminator to Rajasthan Royals by 4 wickets at Ahmedabad. Flower's first season, replacing Sanjay Bangar.
Finished 2nd in league (19 points, NRR 0.482). Beat Punjab Kings in Qualifier 1; beat Punjab Kings again in the final to win RCB's first-ever IPL title after 18 years of the franchise's existence.
2026 season in progress — RCB enter as defending champions. Stats will update as matches complete.

otherCoachingRoles.notes: Led England to the 2010 T20 World Cup title and to the World No.1 Test ranking in 2011.

coaches.name: John Buchanan
coaches.playingCareerSummary: Former Queensland first-class cricketer. Head coach of the all-conquering Australia men's team (1999-2007) that won two ODI World Cups (2003, 2007) and dominated world cricket. Did not play in the IPL.
coaches.bio: The first head coach of Kolkata Knight Riders, appointed ahead of the inaugural 2008 IPL. Came in with an elite pedigree from coaching Australia to World Cup glory but his tenure at KKR became famous for the 'multiple-captains' theory — a rotating captaincy system that clashed with Sourav Ganguly. The fallout hurt team morale and KKR finished 6th in 2008 and bottom of the table in 2009, after which Buchanan was replaced.

perSeason.notes: Finished 6th of 8 with 13 points. Controversial 'multiple-captains' theory conflicted with Sourav Ganguly's captaincy.
Finished 8th (last) with 7 points. Nine consecutive defeats at one stretch. Turbulent season with 'Fake IPL Player' blog controversy. Buchanan was replaced at the end of this season.

otherCoachingRoles.notes: Won 2003 and 2007 ODI World Cups. Oversaw Australia's longest Test winning streak.

coaches.name: Dav Whatmore
coaches.playingCareerSummary: Former Australia Test batter (7 Tests, 1979-80). Sri Lanka-born; emigrated to Australia as a child. Best known as the head coach who guided Sri Lanka to the 1996 ODI World Cup title.
coaches.bio: KKR head coach from 2010 to 2011. Replaced John Buchanan and immediately stabilised the franchise, taking them to their first playoff appearance in 2011. After 2011 he moved on to become Pakistan's head coach. Widely credited with modernising subcontinent coaching methods during his Sri Lanka tenure.

perSeason.notes: Finished 6th with 14 points (NRR -0.341). Missed playoffs on NRR despite being tied on points with three other teams.
Finished 4th with 16 points. Lost Eliminator to Mumbai Indians after Lakshmipathy Balaji's final over conceded 21 runs. First KKR playoff appearance.

otherCoachingRoles.notes: Two stints; led Sri Lanka to the 1996 ODI World Cup title.
Led Bangladesh through their rise as a Test nation.

coaches.name: Trevor Bayliss
coaches.playingCareerSummary: Former New South Wales first-class batter (1985-1995). Did not play international or IPL cricket. One of the most successful T20 coaches of his generation.
coaches.bio: One of the most successful head coaches in IPL history by titles won (2 — KKR 2012 and 2014). Took KKR from Dav Whatmore and delivered the franchise's first two IPL titles, including a record 200-run chase in the 2014 final. Left after 2015 to become England's head coach, leading them to their 2019 ODI World Cup triumph. Returned to the IPL in 2020 with Sunrisers Hyderabad (2020-2021) and Punjab Kings (2023-2024), but neither stint produced a playoff appearance after the 2020 SRH Qualifier 2 run.

perSeason.notes: Finished 2nd in league (21 pts). Beat Delhi Daredevils in Qualifier 1 by 18 runs; beat CSK in final by 5 wickets. KKR's first IPL title. Manvinder Bisla's 89 in the final.
Finished 7th of 9. Title defence collapsed — did not qualify for playoffs.
Finished 2nd in league (20 pts, NRR 0.418). Beat Kings XI Punjab in Qualifier 1; beat KXIP in final by 3 wickets chasing 200 (Manish Pandey 94). KKR's second IPL title.
Finished 5th with 15 points. Did not qualify for playoffs. Bayliss left after this season to become England head coach.
Finished 3rd (14 pts). Beat RCB in Eliminator; lost Qualifier 2 to Delhi Capitals by 17 runs. Replaced Tom Moody for IPL 2020.
Finished 8th (last) with 6 points. Franchise-worst season at that time. Bayliss was replaced by Tom Moody for 2022.

tenures.notes: Sandwiched between Tom Moody's two SRH stints. Took the team to the playoffs in 2020 before a franchise-worst last-place finish in 2021.

perSeason.notes: Finished 8th in Group B with 12 points (NRR -0.304). First season after replacing Anil Kumble in September 2022.
Finished 9th with 10 points. Bayliss left after this season; Ricky Ponting replaced him for IPL 2025.

otherCoachingRoles.notes: Led Sri Lanka to the 2011 ODI World Cup final.
Won BBL/01 (2011-12) and Champions League T20 2012.
Led England to their first-ever ODI World Cup title in 2019.

coaches.name: Jacques Kallis
coaches.playingCareerSummary: Arguably the greatest all-rounder in cricket history: 166 Tests (13,289 runs at 55.37, 292 wickets), 328 ODIs (11,579 runs, 273 wickets). Played for Royal Challengers Bangalore (2008-2010) and Kolkata Knight Riders (2011-2015) in the IPL — won the 2012 and 2014 titles as a player.
coaches.bio: Stepped up from KKR's batting coach to head coach in 2016 after Trevor Bayliss's departure. Took KKR to the playoffs in three of his four seasons (2016, 2017, 2018) but couldn't take them past Qualifier 2. Left after 2019 and later became South Africa's men's Test team batting consultant.

perSeason.notes: Finished 4th (16 pts, NRR 0.106). Lost Eliminator to Sunrisers Hyderabad by 22 runs.
Finished 4th (16 pts). Beat SRH in Eliminator by 7 wickets (DLS); lost Qualifier 2 to Mumbai Indians by 6 wickets.
Finished 3rd (16 pts, NRR -0.070). Beat Rajasthan Royals in Eliminator by 25 runs; lost Qualifier 2 to Sunrisers Hyderabad by 14 runs.
Finished 5th with 13 points. Did not qualify for playoffs. Kallis was replaced by Brendon McCullum after this season.

otherCoachingRoles.notes: KKR batting coach under Trevor Bayliss before being promoted to head coach.
Worked under Chris Silverwood during the Ashes series.

coaches.name: Brendon McCullum
coaches.playingCareerSummary: Former New Zealand captain and one of T20 cricket's original superstars: 101 Tests, 260 ODIs, 71 T20Is. Famously scored the first IPL century (158* for KKR vs RCB in the 2008 opening match). Played for KKR, Kochi, CSK, Gujarat Lions and RCB in the IPL.
coaches.bio: KKR head coach from 2020 to 2022, returning to the franchise where he announced himself as a T20 icon with that 158* on the IPL's opening night. Took KKR to the 2021 final (lost to CSK) but couldn't qualify for playoffs in 2020 or 2022. Left after 2022 to become England's Test head coach, where his 'Bazball' philosophy transformed England's red-ball cricket.

perSeason.notes: Finished 5th with 14 points (NRR -0.214). Missed playoffs on net run rate. Season played in UAE due to COVID-19.
Finished 4th with 14 points. Beat RCB in Eliminator by 4 wickets; beat DC in Qualifier 2 by 3 wickets; lost final to CSK by 27 runs. Season split across India and UAE legs due to COVID-19.
Finished 7th with 12 points. Did not qualify for playoffs. McCullum left after this season to become England's Test head coach.

otherCoachingRoles.notes: Originator of the 'Bazball' approach; revolutionised England's Test cricket.
Took over as all-format England head coach in 2025 after Matthew Mott's departure.

coaches.name: Chandrakant Pandit
coaches.playingCareerSummary: Former India wicket-keeper batter: 5 Tests, 36 ODIs (1986-1992). Did not play in the IPL. The most successful domestic coach in Indian cricket history, with six Ranji Trophy titles (two with Mumbai, three with Vidarbha, one with Madhya Pradesh).
coaches.bio: KKR head coach from 2023 to 2025. Delivered KKR's third IPL title in 2024 with a dominant campaign — just 3 losses all season, 1st in the table, and the fastest run-chase in an IPL final. Worked alongside mentor Gautam Gambhir in 2024 (a partnership widely credited with the transformation). His disciplinarian reputation made him a contrast to the more player-led McCullum era. Stepped aside after his three-year contract ended in 2025.

perSeason.notes: Finished 7th with 12 points. Did not qualify for playoffs. First season replacing Brendon McCullum.

Finished 1st with 20 points. Lost just 3 matches all season (a joint record for fewest losses in a full IPL). Beat SRH in Qualifier 1 by 8 wickets; beat SRH in final by 8 wickets — the fastest IPL final chase (57 balls remaining). KKR's third IPL title. Mentor: Gautam Gambhir.
Finished 8th with 12 points. Eliminated when the RCB match was abandoned. Mentor: Dwayne Bravo. Pandit's three-year contract ended after this season.

otherCoachingRoles.notes: Ranji Trophy titles in 2002-03 and 2015-16.
Back-to-back Ranji Trophy titles in 2017-18 and 2018-19, plus the Irani Cup.
Ranji Trophy title in 2021-22 — MP's first ever.

coaches.name: Abhishek Nayar
coaches.playingCareerSummary: Former India left-handed all-rounder: 3 ODIs (2009). Long domestic career with Mumbai. Played in the IPL for Mumbai Indians, Kings XI Punjab, Pune Warriors and Rajasthan Royals. Widely regarded as one of the best batting mentors in Indian cricket — credited as a key influence in Rohit Sharma's and KL Rahul's development.
coaches.bio: Kolkata Knight Riders' longest-tenured support-staff member before becoming head coach in 2026. Joined KKR as mentor in 2019, progressed through assistant coach (2020-2023) and batting coach (2024 — the title-winning year under mentor Gautam Gambhir), took a season away as India's senior team assistant in 2025, and returned as KKR head coach in 2026. A central figure in KKR's 2024 title run.

perSeason.notes: Finished 5th with 13 points. Did not qualify for playoffs. Kallis was replaced by Brendon McCullum after this season.

tenures.notes: First KKR coaching role — team mentor under Jacques Kallis.

perSeason.notes: Finished 5th with 14 points (NRR -0.214). Missed playoffs on net run rate. Season played in UAE due to COVID-19.
Finished 4th with 14 points. Beat RCB in Eliminator by 4 wickets; beat DC in Qualifier 2 by 3 wickets; lost final to CSK by 27 runs. Season split across India and UAE legs due to COVID-19.
Finished 7th with 12 points. Did not qualify for playoffs. McCullum left after this season to become England's Test head coach.
Finished 7th with 12 points. Did not qualify for playoffs. First season replacing Brendon McCullum.

tenures.notes: Assistant coach across the Brendon McCullum (2020-2022) and Chandrakant Pandit (2023) eras.

perSeason.notes: Finished 1st with 20 points. Lost just 3 matches all season (a joint record for fewest losses in a full IPL). Beat SRH in Qualifier 1 by 8 wickets; beat SRH in final by 8 wickets — the fastest IPL final chase (57 balls remaining). KKR's third IPL title. Mentor: Gautam Gambhir.

tenures.notes: Promoted to batting coach for the 2024 IPL title campaign under Pandit and mentor Gautam Gambhir.

perSeason.notes: First season as head coach. Stats will update as matches complete. Taking over after Chandrakant Pandit's three-year contract ended.

otherCoachingRoles.notes: Assistant coach under Gautam Gambhir on the senior India team.

coaches.name: Greg Shipperd
coaches.playingCareerSummary: Former first-class batter for Western Australia and Tasmania. Did not play international or IPL cricket. One of Australia's most successful state/BBL coaches.
coaches.bio: Delhi Daredevils' inaugural head coach, serving four seasons from 2008 to 2011. Took DD to the semi-finals in the first two IPL seasons, including a league topping finish in 2009. His tenure ended after a last-place 2011. Best known in Australian domestic cricket for coaching Tasmania and the Melbourne Stars, winning multiple Sheffield Shield and BBL titles.

perSeason.notes: Finished 4th in league (15 pts). Lost Semi-final 1 to Rajasthan Royals by 105 runs at Wankhede. Gautam Gambhir's 534 runs led the batting.
Finished 1st in league (20 pts) — their best ever league finish. Lost semi-final to Deccan Chargers by 6 wickets. Season played in South Africa. Captain: Virender Sehwag.
Finished 5th with 14 points (NRR 0.021). Missed playoffs on net run rate in a four-way tie.
Finished 10th (last) with 9 points. Franchise-worst performance. Shipperd left after the season.

tenures.notes: Franchise named 'Delhi Daredevils' for the entire Shipperd era. Rebranded to 'Delhi Capitals' only in 2019.

otherCoachingRoles.notes: Sheffield Shield and ING Cup titles.
Multiple domestic titles including Sheffield Shield and Ryobi One-Day Cup.
Multiple BBL finals appearances.

coaches.name: Eric Simons
coaches.playingCareerSummary: Former South Africa medium-pacer: 23 ODIs between 1992-1994. Widely respected bowling coach post-retirement.
coaches.bio: One of the most experienced bowling coaches in world cricket with a remarkable career arc across three IPL franchises. Delhi Daredevils head coach for 2012 and 2013. Joined Chennai Super Kings as bowling consultant in 2018 — a role he held for seven seasons alongside lead bowling coaches L Balaji and Dwayne Bravo — before being promoted to CSK's lead bowling coach from IPL 2025 after Bravo's departure. Also long-serving bowling coach for South Africa and India at the international level.

perSeason.notes: Finished 1st in league (22 pts) — franchise-best league finish. Lost Qualifier 1 to KKR by 18 runs; lost Qualifier 2 to CSK by 86 runs. Finished 3rd overall.
Finished 9th (last) with 6 points (NRR -0.848). Captain Mahela Jayawardene couldn't arrest the collapse. Simons was replaced after this season.
Finished 7th of 8 with 10 points. Franchise's inaugural season during CSK's suspension.
Finished 2nd in league (20 pts). Beat MI in Qualifier 1 by 20 runs; lost final to MI by 1 run. Franchise's final season — disbanded when CSK returned in 2018. Captain: Steve Smith.

tenures.notes: Bowling coach under Stephen Fleming at RPS for both seasons, including the 2017 final run.

perSeason.notes: Return from 2016-17 suspension. Finished 2nd in league (18 pts). Qualifier 1 W vs SRH by 2 wickets, final W vs SRH by 8 wickets (Shane Watson 117* Player of the Match). Third IPL title.
Finished 2nd (18 pts, tied with MI and DC on NRR). Qualifier 1 L vs MI by 6 wkts, Qualifier 2 W vs DC by 6 wkts, lost final to MI by 1 run.
First season CSK failed to qualify for playoffs. Finished 7th (12 pts). Played in UAE due to COVID-19.
Finished 2nd in league (18 pts). Qualifier 1 W vs DC by 4 wickets, final W vs KKR by 27 runs in Dubai. Fourth IPL title.
Finished 9th of 10 (8 pts). Jadeja stepped down as captain mid-season; Dhoni resumed leadership.
Finished 2nd (17 pts). Qualifier 1 W vs GT by 15 runs, final W vs GT by 5 wickets (DLS) in rain-affected three-day final. Fifth IPL title, equalling MI.
Missed playoffs on NRR tie-breaker. Finished 5th (14 pts). Dhoni handed captaincy to Ruturaj Gaikwad before the season.

tenures.notes: Bowling consultant alongside head bowling coach (Balaji 2018-21, Bravo 2022-24).

perSeason.notes: Franchise-worst: finished 10th (last) with 8 pts — the first time CSK ended at the bottom of the table.
2026 season in progress — stats will update as matches complete.

tenures.notes: Promoted from bowling consultant to lead bowling coach when Bravo left for KKR.

otherCoachingRoles.notes: Head coach 2002-2004, then bowling coach in his second stint.
India's bowling coach under Gary Kirsten and then Duncan Fletcher.

coaches.name: Paddy Upton
coaches.playingCareerSummary: Did not play first-class or international cricket. Pioneer in mental conditioning and sports psychology; published author on coaching.
coaches.bio: One of the IPL's most widely-travelled head coaches, with two distinct stints at Rajasthan Royals (2013-2015, 2018-2019) sandwiching a spell at Delhi Daredevils (2016-2017). Replaced Monty Desai at RR for 2013 and immediately took the side to the Qualifier 2 playoffs, including a franchise-record 13 straight home wins. Returned to RR in 2018 after their 2016-17 suspension ended, taking them back to the Eliminator playoffs. Previously best known as mental conditioning coach of the 2011 ODI World Cup winning India side under Gary Kirsten.

perSeason.notes: Finished 3rd in league (20 pts). Beat Sunrisers Hyderabad in Eliminator by 4 wickets; lost Qualifier 2 to Mumbai Indians by 4 wickets. Upton's first season after replacing Monty Desai. Also set a record of 13 straight home wins.
Finished 5th with 14 points (NRR 0.060). Did not qualify for playoffs.
Finished 4th with 16 points. Lost Eliminator to Royal Challengers Bangalore by 72 runs. Final season of Upton's first stint — RR was then suspended for 2016-2017.

tenures.notes: First stint at RR. Took over from Monty Desai and led RR from consecutive bottom-half finishes to a playoff appearance in 2013 (including a franchise-record 13 straight home wins). 2016-2017 RR suspension ended the first stint.

perSeason.notes: Finished 6th with 14 points (NRR -0.155). Missed playoffs on net run rate — tied on points with Mumbai Indians. Rahul Dravid was appointed as batting mentor this year.
Finished 6th with 12 points (NRR -0.512). Upton and TA Sekar were replaced at the end of the season; Ricky Ponting was appointed for 2018.
Return from suspension. Finished 4th with 14 points (NRR -0.250). Lost Eliminator to Kolkata Knight Riders by 25 runs.
Finished 7th with 11 points (NRR -0.449). Upton left after this season; Andrew McDonald replaced him.

tenures.notes: Second stint at RR, after the franchise's return from the 2016-2017 suspension. Replaced by Andrew McDonald for 2020.

otherCoachingRoles.notes: Part of the Gary Kirsten era that won the 2011 ODI World Cup.
Won the BBL|05 title (2015-16).
RR head coach stint — to be tracked separately under RR research.

coaches.name: Hemang Badani
coaches.playingCareerSummary: Former India left-handed middle-order batter and slow left-arm orthodox bowler: 4 Tests and 40 ODIs (2000-2004). Scored 867 ODI runs at 33.34, highlighted by a century vs Australia at Pune in 2001-02. Represented Tamil Nadu in domestic cricket. Did not play in the IPL; his playing career predated the league.
coaches.bio: Long coaching journey that began at Sunrisers Hyderabad as fielding coach (2022-2023) and batting coach (2024 — the final-reaching season), before being promoted to Delhi Capitals head coach for IPL 2025 (replacing Ricky Ponting). Retained for 2026 with Kevin Pietersen as mentor.

perSeason.notes: Finished 8th with 12 points (NRR -0.379). Moody's second stint at SRH; he stepped down on 2 September 2022 after this season.
Finished 10th (last) with 8 points (NRR -0.590). Lara's only IPL head-coaching season. Replaced by Daniel Vettori for IPL 2024.

tenures.notes: SRH fielding coach for two seasons under Tom Moody and Brian Lara.

perSeason.notes: Finished 2nd in league (17 pts). Qualifier 1 loss to KKR; Qualifier 2 W vs Rajasthan Royals; lost final to KKR by 8 wickets. SRH's second IPL final after 2018. Vettori's first season replacing Brian Lara.

tenures.notes: Promoted to batting coach for IPL 2024 under Daniel Vettori — part of the final-reaching squad. Left after this season to become Delhi Capitals head coach.

perSeason.notes: Finished 5th in Group B with 15 points. Last team eliminated from IPL 2025. Kevin Pietersen served as team mentor.
2026 season in progress — stats will update as matches complete. Venugopal Rao is Director of Cricket; Kevin Pietersen continues as team mentor; Munaf Patel is bowling coach.

otherCoachingRoles.notes: Head coach since TNPL's inaugural season; multiple titles.

coaches.name: Tom Moody
coaches.playingCareerSummary: Former Australia batting all-rounder: 8 Tests, 76 ODIs (1987-1999). Part of the 1987 and 1999 ODI World Cup winning Australia squads. Did not play in the IPL.
coaches.bio: One of the most prolific and successful T20 franchise head coaches ever — 11 IPL seasons across two franchises with one championship. Inaugural head coach of Kings XI Punjab (2008-2010), taking them to the 2008 semi-finals. Joined Sunrisers Hyderabad in 2013 as their inaugural head coach and served two stints (2013-2019, 2022), delivering SRH's maiden IPL title in 2016 (beating RCB by 8 runs in the final) and another final appearance in 2018. Also served as Director of Cricket at SRH during 2015-2019. Stepped down from his second stint on 2 September 2022 and was replaced by Brian Lara.

perSeason.notes: Finished 2nd in league (20 pts) — franchise-best league finish at the time. Lost Semi-final 2 to Chennai Super Kings by 9 wickets in Mumbai. Captain: Yuvraj Singh.
Finished 5th with 14 points. Missed playoffs (NRR -0.483). Season played in South Africa.
Finished 8th (last) with 8 points. Franchise-worst season; Moody left after this.
Inaugural SRH season (replacing Deccan Chargers). Finished 4th in league (20 pts). Lost Eliminator to Rajasthan Royals by 4 wickets.
Finished 6th with 12 points.
Finished 6th with 14 points (NRR -0.239).

Finished 3rd in league (16 pts, NRR +0.577). Beat KKR in Eliminator; beat Gujarat Lions in Qualifier 2; beat RCB by 8 runs in final. SRH's maiden IPL title — first team to win the Eliminator, Qualifier 2 and Final in the same playoff run. David Warner Orange Cap winner with 848 runs.
Finished 3rd (17 pts). Lost Eliminator to KKR (DLS) after posting 128/7 in rain-curtailed match.

Finished 1st in league (18 pts, NRR 0.284). Lost Qualifier 1 to CSK by 2 wickets; beat KKR in Qualifier 2 by 14 runs; lost final to CSK by 8 wickets. Kane Williamson replaced David Warner as captain after the latter's Cape Town Test ball-tampering ban.
Finished 4th with 12 points (NRR +0.577). Lost Eliminator to Delhi Capitals by 2 wickets.

tenures.notes: First stint at SRH — seven seasons. Delivered the franchise's maiden IPL title in 2016 and a second final appearance in 2018. SRH's most successful coach.

perSeason.notes: Finished 8th with 12 points (NRR -0.379). Moody's second stint at SRH; he stepped down on 2 September 2022 after this season.

tenures.notes: Second stint — returned after Trevor Bayliss's 2020-2021 tenure, but stepped down on 2 September 2022. Replaced by Brian Lara for 2023.

otherCoachingRoles.notes: County Championship title in 2004.
Led Sri Lanka to the 2007 ODI World Cup final.

coaches.name: Michael Bevan
coaches.playingCareerSummary: Former Australia middle-order batter widely regarded as the best ODI 'finisher' of his era: 18 Tests, 232 ODIs (6912 runs at 53.58). Two-time ODI World Cup winner (1999, 2003). Did not play in the IPL.
coaches.bio: Kings XI Punjab head coach for IPL 2011, a single-season tenure. Took over from Tom Moody and finished 5th, narrowly missing the playoffs. Was replaced by Adam Gilchrist the following year.

perSeason.notes: Finished 5th with 14 points. Missed playoffs. Single-season tenure replacing Tom Moody.

coaches.name: Adam Gilchrist
coaches.playingCareerSummary: Former Australia wicket-keeper batter and three-time ODI World Cup winner (1999, 2003, 2007): 96 Tests (5570 runs at 47.60, 379 dismissals), 287 ODIs. Played for Deccan Chargers (2008-2010, winning the 2009 IPL title as captain) and Kings XI Punjab (2011-2013).
coaches.bio: Took on the unusual dual role of captain-cum-head-coach at Kings XI Punjab for IPL 2012. The experiment was short — KXIP finished 6th and Gilchrist was replaced as coach by Darren Lehmann for 2013 (though Gilchrist continued as captain). His only IPL head-coaching stint.

perSeason.notes: Finished 6th with 16 points. Player-coach role: Gilchrist was simultaneously KXIP captain and head coach. Did not qualify for playoffs.

tenures.notes: Player-coach role. Gilchrist was the KXIP captain simultaneously.

coaches.name: Darren Lehmann
coaches.playingCareerSummary: Former Australia middle-order batter: 27 Tests (1798 runs at 44.95), 117 ODIs. Two-time ODI World Cup winner (1999, 2003). Played for Rajasthan Royals in IPL 2008.
coaches.bio: Kings XI Punjab head coach for IPL 2013, a single-season tenure in which KXIP finished 6th. Left IPL coaching to become Australia's national head coach from June 2013 to 2018, winning the 2015 ODI World Cup at home.

perSeason.notes: Finished 1st in league (15 pts). Beat DD in semi-final by 6 wickets; beat RCB in final by 6 runs at Johannesburg. Inaugural title for the franchise. Season played in South Africa.
Finished 2nd in league (16 pts). Lost both playoff matches — semi-final to CSK and 3rd-place playoff to RCB.
Finished 7th of 10 with 12 points. Home record particularly poor.
Finished 8th of 9 with 9 points. Franchise terminated by BCCI after this season.

tenures.notes: Four-season stint during which Lehmann delivered Deccan's only IPL title in 2009 (in South Africa). The franchise declined afterwards and was terminated after the 2012 season.

perSeason.notes: Finished 6th with 16 points. Did not qualify for playoffs. Lehmann left after this season to become Australia's national head coach.

otherCoachingRoles.notes: Won the 2009 IPL title — will be tracked when defunct franchises are added in Phase 4.
Won the 2015 ODI World Cup at home. Stepped down after the 2018 Sandpaper-gate scandal in South Africa.

coaches.name: Virender Sehwag
coaches.playingCareerSummary: One of India's greatest openers and a revolutionary T20 hitter: 104 Tests (8586 runs at 49.34 with two triple-hundreds), 251 ODIs. Played for Delhi Daredevils (2008-2013) and Kings XI Punjab (2014-2015) in the IPL.
coaches.bio: Took charge of Kings XI Punjab in January 2017 as 'Head of Cricket Operations and Strategy' after Sanjay Bangar stepped down — a role that effectively made him head coach for the 2017 season. KXIP finished 5th, narrowly missing the playoffs. He continued in the mentor/DoC role for several subsequent seasons but was succeeded as head coach by Brad Hodge for 2018.

perSeason.notes: Finished 5th with 14 points (NRR -0.009). Just missed playoffs by net run rate. Officially held the title 'Head of Cricket Operations and Strategy' after Sanjay Bangar stepped down — functioned as head coach with J. Arunkumar as batting coach. KXIP did not appoint a separately-titled head coach for this season.

tenures.notes: Official title was 'Head of Cricket Operations and Strategy'. KXIP had no separately-appointed head coach in 2017 — Sehwag functioned as head coach in practice.

otherCoachingRoles.notes: Continued as mentor / Director of Cricket operations after stepping away from head coaching duties.

coaches.name: Brad Hodge
coaches.playingCareerSummary: Former Australia batter: 6 Tests, 25 ODIs, 15 T20Is. Extensive domestic and T20 franchise career. Played for KKR (2008-2010, 2014), Kochi Tuskers Kerala (2011) and Rajasthan Royals (2012-2015) in the IPL.
coaches.bio: Kings XI Punjab head coach for IPL 2018. KXIP finished 7th, and Hodge was replaced after a single season. Previously coached in the Big Bash and was an assistant at Rajasthan Royals.

perSeason.notes: Finished 2nd in league (18 pts) — inaugural-season playoff qualification. Lost Qualifier 1 to RCB by 4 wickets; lost Qualifier 2 to Sunrisers Hyderabad.
Finished 7th of 8 with 8 points. Franchise's final season — disbanded after 2017.

tenures.notes: Head coach across both of Gujarat Lions' IPL seasons. Took them to Qualifier 2 in their inaugural 2016 campaign before a poor 2017. Franchise was disbanded after 2017.

perSeason.notes: Finished 7th with 12 points. Missed playoffs. Single-season tenure; Mike Hesson replaced him for 2019.

otherCoachingRoles.notes: Won the BBL|06 title (2016-17).

coaches.name: Mike Hesson
coaches.playingCareerSummary: Did not play first-class or international cricket. Rose through the coaching ranks in New Zealand and became one of the most respected strategists in the modern game.
coaches.bio: New Zealand's former head coach (2012-2018) who led NZ to their first ever World Cup final in 2015. In the IPL: Kings XI Punjab head coach (2019, one season) and Royal Challengers Bangalore Director of Cricket Operations (2020-2023), also serving as interim head coach during the 2021 UAE leg after Simon Katich stepped down.

perSeason.notes: Finished 6th with 12 points (NRR -0.251). Missed playoffs. Hesson later moved to RCB as Director of Cricket (2020-2023).
Finished 4th with 14 points (NRR -0.172). Lost Eliminator to Sunrisers Hyderabad by 6 wickets. Played in UAE due to COVID-19.

Finished 3rd with 18 points. Katich stepped down mid-season citing personal reasons (August 21, 2021), after which Director of Cricket Mike Hesson took over as interim head coach for the UAE leg and the playoffs. Lost Eliminator to KKR by 4 wickets. Season split across India and UAE legs due to COVID-19.
Finished 4th. Beat LSG in Eliminator by 14 runs; lost Qualifier 2 to Rajasthan Royals by 7 wickets.
Finished 6th with 14 points. Did not qualify for playoffs. Replaced by Andy Flower ahead of IPL 2024.

tenures.notes: Director of Cricket Operations 2020-2023. Also served as interim head coach during the 2021 UAE leg after Simon Katich stepped down in August 2021.

otherCoachingRoles.notes: Led NZ to the 2015 ODI World Cup final — their first ever World Cup final.

otherCoachingRoles.role: Director of Cricket / Interim Head Coach
otherCoachingRoles.notes: DoC throughout; interim head coach for the 2021 UAE leg after Simon Katich stepped down mid-season.

coaches.name: Anil Kumble
coaches.playingCareerSummary: India's highest wicket-taker in Tests: 132 Tests (619 wickets) and 271 ODIs (337 wickets). Former India captain. Played for Royal Challengers Bangalore in the IPL (2008-2010), captaining them to the 2009 final.
coaches.bio: Head coach and Director of Cricket Operations of the Punjab franchise from 2020 to 2022 — a tenure that spanned the franchise's rebrand from Kings XI Punjab to Punjab Kings in February 2021. All three of his seasons ended in a 6th-place finish without a playoff berth, and he was replaced by Trevor Bayliss in September 2022. Previously head coach of the India national team (2016-2017), a stint that ended after a publicised rift with captain Virat Kohli.

perSeason.notes: Finished 6th with 12 points. Final season under the 'Kings XI Punjab' name — the franchise rebranded to 'Punjab Kings' on 17 February 2021.

tenures.notes: Final season under the 'Kings XI Punjab' name. Kumble also served as Director of Cricket Operations.

perSeason.notes: First season under the 'Punjab Kings' name. Finished 6th with 12 points.
Finished 6th with 14 points (NRR 0.126). Missed playoffs. Kumble was replaced by Trevor Bayliss in September 2022.

tenures.notes: First two seasons under the rebranded 'Punjab Kings' name. Kumble continued as Director of Cricket Operations alongside head-coaching duties across both stints.

otherCoachingRoles.notes: One-year tenure; stepped down after the 2017 ICC Champions Trophy amid reports of a rift with captain Virat Kohli.
MI's 'chief mentor' during their first IPL title win; to be added as a support-staff entry in Phase 2.

coaches.name: Shane Warne
coaches.playingCareerSummary: One of the greatest leg-spinners in cricket history and an all-time great of the game: 145 Tests (708 wickets at 25.41), 194 ODIs. Played for Rajasthan Royals in the IPL (2008-2011), captaining the side that won the inaugural 2008 title.
coaches.bio: Rajasthan Royals' inaugural head coach and captain in 2008 — the original captain-coach who masterminded RR's inaugural IPL title with a squad built around young, unheralded Indian talent. Remained captain-coach through 2011, then continued as non-playing head coach for 2012 after retiring from active IPL cricket. His revolutionary tactical approach, embrace of data, and trust in youngsters defined the first chapter of Rajasthan Royals' identity. Replaced by Paddy Upton for IPL 2013.

perSeason.notes: Finished 1st in league (22 pts). Beat Delhi Daredevils in Semi-final 1 by 105 runs; beat Chennai Super Kings in final by 3 wickets at DY Patil Stadium. Franchise's only IPL title and the inaugural IPL championship. Operational/team coach Monty Desai assisted Warne.
Finished 6th with 13 points. Season played in South Africa. Did not qualify for playoffs.
Finished 7th with 12 points (NRR -0.514).
Finished 6th with 13 points. Warne's final season as player; retired from IPL after this campaign.

coaches.name: Andrew McDonald
coaches.playingCareerSummary: Former Australia all-rounder: 4 Tests, 3 T20Is. Played for Delhi Daredevils (2009) and Royal Challengers Bangalore (2010) in the IPL before transitioning into coaching. Current head coach of the Australia senior men's cricket team.
coaches.bio: Rajasthan Royals head coach for IPL 2020 — a single-season tenure during the COVID-affected UAE season. RR finished bottom of the table with 12 points and McDonald was let go in early 2021 despite a three-year contract, with Kumar Sangakkara (then appointed as Director of Cricket) taking over as acting head coach instead of a direct replacement. McDonald went on to become Australia's head coach in 2022, a role he still holds.

coaches.name: Kumar Sangakkara

Made way for Rahul Dravid for IPL 2025 but returned for 2026 after Dravid's single-season stint — his second tenure. Working partnership with Trevor Penney, Shane Bond and Vikram Rathour defines the current RR coaching setup.

coaches.bio: Rajasthan Royals head coach for IPL 2025, reuniting with the franchise where he had captained in 2012-2013 and mentored in 2014-2015. Took charge after Kumar Sangakkara's four-year tenure but RR had a poor 2025 campaign, finishing 9th. Left after a single season and Sangakkara returned for 2026. Best known for his transformative tenure as India national team head coach (2021-2024), winning the 2024 T20 World Cup.

perSeason.notes: Finished 9th with 8 points (NRR -0.549). Second team eliminated from IPL 2025. Single-season tenure; Dravid left after this and Sangakkara returned for 2026.

otherCoachingRoles.notes: Player-captain 2012-2013, mentor 2014-2015.

coaches.name: Brian Lara
coaches.playingCareerSummary: One of the greatest batters in cricket history: 131 Tests (11,953 runs at 52.88), 299 ODIs (10,405 runs). Holds the record for the highest individual Test score (400*) and first-class score (501*). Did not play in the IPL (retired in 2007 before the league began).
coaches.bio: Sunrisers Hyderabad head coach for IPL 2023 — his only IPL head-coaching role. Took over from Tom Moody and presided over a franchise-worst last-place finish (4W-10L). coaches.name: Ashish Nehra
coaches.playingCareerSummary: Former India left-arm fast bowler: 17 Tests, 120 ODIs (157 wickets), 27 T20Is.
